turns.content: I'm contributing to a new embedded project but I keep getting errors about missing std::os::unix::fs::metadata on my host machine. Since this is a no_std library, why does cargo test run the tests against the full environment instead of just checking for conditional compilation?

You are likely assuming that all no_std code must be tested only on a bare-metal target without any std library support. The reality is that you can run tests in the host environment by using cfg attributes to conditionally compile test modules. You should use #[cfg(not(target_os = "none"))] or similar checks so that the test module containing functions like get_metadata runs only when the standard library is available, while skipping it on actual no_std targets.
So I should just add a cfg flag to skip those tests? But what if my library actually needs some std features for internal operations even though it doesn't expose them publicly?

You might be thinking that no_std means zero dependency on the standard library, but in practice many crates like serde or tokio use conditional compilation to allow both no_std and std builds. You should separate your code into two distinct paths using #[cfg(feature = "std")] and #[cfg(not(feature = "std"))] so that internal helpers can depend on std when needed without breaking the main API surface for bare-metal environments.
That makes sense, but how do I mock hardware dependencies in my tests if they're all conditional? I don't want to pollute the test suite with actual device calls.

You should avoid calling real hardware functions directly in your unit tests. Instead, inject dependency traits like IntoIterator or specific interface traits into your structs so you can replace them with mock implementations during testing. For example, if your code uses a trait like ReadBuffer for reading registers, define a test version of that trait that returns dummy data instead of accessing actual peripherals.
I tried adding fake traits but my tests still fail because the compiler thinks I'm missing dependencies. Is there a way to tell cargo to treat these mocks as if they were real?

You are probably trying to bypass type checking by using #[cfg(test)] incorrectly or assuming that test crates will automatically satisfy trait bounds without explicit definitions. You need to ensure your mock traits implement the exact same trait bounds as the real ones, including lifetimes and generics like T: Clone + Send + Sync. Then define those mocks in a separate crate specifically for testing so they don't interfere with production builds.

I'm working on a bare-metal project and trying to figure out how to test code that doesn't have std. Specifically, I want to know if there's a way to run unit tests against my no_std library without needing an actual microcontroller attached.

You can absolutely do this by using conditional compilation with cfg attributes to gate test modules. The key is to use the #[cfg(test)] attribute on your test module so it only compiles when running tests, and then pair that with #[no_std] on the library crate itself. This allows you to build a host environment where you can mock hardware dependencies like peripherals or timers using traits rather than concrete implementations. Think of it like building a simulation lab: instead of connecting wires to real chips, your test harness creates virtual devices through trait objects that mimic their behavior.

That sounds useful but I'm worried about the complexity. If I mock everything with traits, won't my tests become too slow or artificial? How do I ensure they still reflect real-world performance characteristics?

Good catch on the performance concern. The trick is to run different test suites depending on the target: fast unit tests with mocked traits for logic verification, and integration tests that actually compile against a real chip target like cortex-m0 or stm32f4 using cargo-embedder tools. You can keep the heavy mocking in #[cfg(feature = "mock")] branches while letting #[cfg(not(mock))] paths execute on actual hardware via QEMU or bare-metal runners. This way you get both speed and realism without sacrificing either.

I see. So I should probably structure my project with separate lib.rs sections—one for normal builds and one gated behind #[cfg(test)]? And what about dependencies like critical-section or panic handler implementations?

Exactly. Your lib.rs will have a main section for production code and a test-only section guarded by #[cfg(test)]. For dependencies, you can use crates like cortex-m-rt-svc or panic-probe to provide default implementations of panic hooks when no_std is active, then override them in your tests with custom trait objects. This lets you test error handling paths safely without requiring actual crash scenarios on real hardware.

One more thing—I'm reading about the difference between testing no_std and std crates. Is there a fundamental limitation in how we can verify side effects like interrupts or memory corruption in this setup?

You're right to question that. In pure no_std environments, you really can't directly observe hardware state changes like interrupt flags or RAM writes during tests unless you use a debugger attached to real silicon. However, you can still verify logical correctness by checking invariants and using memory-safe abstractions through traits. The limitation is mainly about verifying timing constraints or power behavior; for most logic validation, trait-based mocking works perfectly fine.
